ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for croissant as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for specific baked goods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium croissant weighing approximately 60 g, as commonly served at a bakery or café in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Classic Croissant — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Classic Croissant (cr_id G00010807). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for croissant (FDC ID 174987) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins set to -1 where neither HPB nor USDA report values for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 67 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for croissant (white flour, butter-laminated). Classic Croissant is a standard butter-laminated croissant made from refined wheat flour; this GI value is the closest defensible analogous match. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 67 × 45.8 × 0.60 / 100 = 67 × 27.48 / 100 ≈ 18.4, rounded to 18.3 for a 60 g serving (27.48 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (118 mg/100 g) and high sugar (11.26 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit croissant consumption to occasional small portions due to the high sodium content of 300 mg per 100 g, the unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, and the added sugar load; avoid pairing with sweet fillings, jams, or spreads that further elevate sugar and caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (45.8 g/100 g), high sugar (11.26 g/100 g), low dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g), and GI of 67 producing a high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat croissants as an occasional food only; if consumed, limit to half a croissant (approximately 30 g) and pair with a protein source such as egg or cheese to attenuate the postprandial glucose spike driven by the high refined-carbohydrate and low-fibre profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~11.5 g/100 g from butter lamination, trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g, cholesterol 67 mg/100 g, and sodium 300 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular croissant consumption due to the high saturated fat content from butter lamination (~11.5 g/100 g), the presence of trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g), and elevated sodium; these factors collectively raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, both key CVD risk drivers.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) from butter lamination promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or very rarely consume croissants, as the high saturated fat from butter lamination and likely trace trans fat from commercial production are pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate joint inflammation; no anti-inflammatory nutrients are present to offset this risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Classic Croissant is a flaky, buttery, crescent-shaped pastry of French origin, widely consumed in Singapore as a breakfast item, snack, or teatime treat. It is made from a laminated yeast-leavened dough in which layers of cold butter are repeatedly folded into the dough through a process called laminage, creating hundreds of thin, alternating layers of dough and fat that puff and separate during baking to produce the characteristic light, airy, and crisp texture. The exterior is golden-brown and shatteringly crisp, while the interior is soft, honeycomb-like, and richly flavoured with butter. Per 100 g, the Classic Croissant provides 406 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 21 g fat (of which approximately 11.5 g is saturated fat from butter), 45.8 g carbohydrate, 11.26 g sugar, 2.6 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The high refined-carbohydrate content, low fibre, and elevated saturated fat make it a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 67 (medium GI), but the glycemic load per standard 60 g serving is approximately 18, placing it in the high glycemic load category. In Singapore, croissants are commonly sold at bakeries, cafés, and hotel breakfast buffets, and may be served plain or filled with ham, cheese, chocolate, or almond paste. The Chinese name 牛角包 (niú jiǎo bāo, literally 'ox-horn bun') is widely used in Singapore and across Chinese-speaking communities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a croissant?
How much fat is in a croissant?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Classic Croissant (cr_id G00010807) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central croissant entry (FDC ID 174987) as the closest approved-source analogue. GI sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database as an analogous estimate for a standard butter-laminated croissant.
notes: Saturated fat of 11.5 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174987, Croissant, butter) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; this is consistent with the health_suitability estimate in the input record. Trans fat of 0.3 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central for commercial butter-laminated croissant; actual trans fat may vary by manufacturer. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA FoodData Central values not directly mapped due to formulation differences. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Classic Croissant and detailed values were not mapped from USDA for this specific preparation. The input record classifies this food under food_group 'Desserts' and food_subgroup 'Cakes'; the subgroup has been updated to 'Pastries' as croissants are laminated pastries, not cakes. The input cultural.primary_cuisine field listed 'Malay' which appears to be a data entry error; croissants are of French origin and are consumed across all ethnic groups in Singapore. Multilingual names have been provided for all four mandatory Singaporean language groups (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this food is consumed in Singapore across all communities. Halal status is marked false as standard croissants contain butter and eggs which may not be from halal-certified sources; consumers should verify with the specific bakery.
